# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Cohort AI Engineering Course

## 0. Assumptions

This brief is Mode A (full-brief mode): all 8 fields were supplied. No defaulting was required. The table below records the values as interpreted for transparency.

| Field | Value from brief |
|---|---|
| Sector | Cohort-based education (Rider 1) |
| Outcome | 120 seats sold across 3 cohorts (40/cohort) at €1,200/seat = €144,000 gross revenue over 8 months |
| Audience | Senior software engineers (5–15 yrs experience) at European companies; feel AI adoption pressure; skeptical of hype; earning €60–100k; will invest if ROI is concrete |
| Competition | Large platforms (Udemy/Coursera, €20–50/course, unlimited marketing budget); bootcamps (€5k+, varied spend); a small number of cohort courses from US-based teachers ($2k+, €5–15k/cohort on ads). No specific competitor names provided; using category-level description per anti-fabrication rule. |
| Budget | €20,000 over 8 months (~€2,500/month). Competitor cohort courses spend €5–15k per cohort on ads (~€2–6k/month per active competitor). |
| Existing channels | Newsletter: 4k subs, 38% open rate. LinkedIn: 14k followers, avg 8k post reach. GitHub: 12k stars on popular OSS project. Pilot cohort: 15 students, 9/15 referrals from founder's existing network. Testimonials and case studies available. |
| Bottleneck | Scale past existing network — reach engineers who don't follow her yet but would trust her on first encounter |
| Time window | 8 months, 3 cohort launches at ~2.5-month intervals |
| Capacity | Founder: 8h/week on marketing (full-time teaching during cohorts). Ops: 10h/week. |

*Confirm or adjust any row and I will re-run the affected stages.*

---

## 1. Campaign Ideas

### Concept A — The Open Curriculum
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative + Community-Build
**One-line thesis:** Publish the entire course curriculum and session recordings as a free public resource, then sell the room — the peers, the cohort bond, the live feedback, the alumni network — making the free-information competitors irrelevant.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder presence, community node, alumni word of mouth) with Tier 2 earned-media pull from the open curriculum itself
**Authenticity hook:** The actual curriculum lives on GitHub, verifiable and forkable — the same credibility signal as the founder's OSS project. The contrast with courses that hide their syllabi behind a paywall is immediate.
**Minimum viable version:** Publish one full module (slides, exercises, annotated references) as a GitHub repo + companion newsletter issue. Measure inbound DMs and application inquiries within two weeks.

---

### Concept B — The Practitioner's Proof
**Archetype:** Founder-Story Arc + Referral Flywheel
**One-line thesis:** The founder documents her own AI-assisted engineering workflow in public — real tasks, real diffs, real failures — turning her work log into the most credible proof-of-value artifact in the category.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder LinkedIn + newsletter) → Tier 2 (engineer communities share the work log organically)
**Authenticity hook:** Actual pull requests from the founder's real projects, shared as annotated case studies. Reviewable, forkable, falsifiable — nothing an ad budget can replicate.
**Minimum viable version:** One "week in AI-assisted engineering" newsletter issue with 3 real tasks, what worked, what failed, and one specific lesson. Publish and measure open rate, reply rate, and shares over 2 weeks.

---

### Concept C — The Alumni Referral Engine
**Archetype:** Referral Flywheel
**One-line thesis:** Activate the 15 pilot alumni as a structured referral network — each one is a trusted peer vouching into their own professional circles, where a word from a known colleague converts faster than any ad impression.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (word of mouth, direct referral from alumni) with a structured incentive or recognition mechanic
**Authenticity hook:** The pilot cohort's 9/15 referral rate proves the social proof is already there. The campaign makes it systematic and legible rather than accidental.
**Minimum viable version:** Email all 15 pilot alumni with a specific, low-friction ask: share one LinkedIn post about what they built during the course, tag the founder, and mention the next cohort is open. Track inbound inquiries that mention a name.

---

### Concept D — The Engineering Community Build
**Archetype:** Community-Build + Earned Media
**One-line thesis:** Launch a Slack or Discord for European engineers navigating AI adoption — free to join, founder-hosted, anchored in practical peer exchange — and let the community be the most visible ongoing proof that the course delivers real practitioners, not hype.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (community node + founder daily presence) with Tier 2 (earned media from community artifacts — threads, debates, case studies shared outward)
**Authenticity hook:** A real community has real arguments and real expertise visible to outsiders. Guest threads from working engineers carry more credibility than any testimonial page.
**Minimum viable version:** Seed with 20 personal invites from the founder's existing network before public announcement. Founder posts daily for 14 days. Measure whether replies come from people outside the founder's prior network.

---

### Concept E — The Search-Capture Play
**Archetype:** Awareness Push (demand capture variant)
**One-line thesis:** Dominate the long-tail search queries senior engineers use when they are evaluating whether to invest in AI upskilling — "AI tooling for engineers who don't want hype," "practical AI pair programming," "AI-assisted code review for seniors" — turning intent into inbound applications.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (SEO content targeting intent queries) + Tier 1 (newsletter as the conversion endpoint)
**Authenticity hook:** The content is written by someone who actually does the work. Search engines and LLM-powered search increasingly surface authoritative practitioner voices; the founder's GitHub reputation and newsletter credibility are rankable signals.
**Minimum viable version:** Publish two long-form newsletter issues structured around the exact phrasing senior engineers use when they search ("I already know how to code — what does AI actually change about my workflow?"). UTM-tag, measure newsletter signups and application inquiries per issue.

---

## 2. Selected Concept

The brief's bottleneck — "reach engineers who don't follow her yet but would trust her on first encounter" — maps precisely onto **Concepts A and B combined**, with **Concept C** as a parallel sub-campaign from day one.

**Primary campaign: Concept B (The Practitioner's Proof)** — the founder's documented AI-engineering workflow is the trust accelerator for cold audiences. It is the thing that can travel through engineer networks, get shared in Slack threads and Hacker News comments, and produce trust in people who have never heard of the course.

**Supporting campaign: Concept C (Alumni Referral Engine)** — activated in parallel from week 1. The 15 pilot alumni are an existing warm asset that converts faster than any cold channel.

**Secondary layer: Concept A (Open Curriculum) as an SEO and credibility anchor** — one free module published to GitHub + newsletter mid-campaign. This gives the earned media and search-capture function without requiring a separate campaign build.

The three work together: the practitioner's proof content attracts cold audiences who trust the founder's demonstrated expertise; the open curriculum removes the price-before-trust objection; the alumni network converts the interested into enrolled.

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

**Your asymmetry is mild-to-severe.** Your €2,500/month sits at the low end against cohort course competitors spending €5–15k/cohort on ads; compared to Udemy/Coursera with effectively unlimited marketing budgets you are at categorical. However, your actual competition for the 40-seat cohort is not Udemy (they sell a different product at a different price point to a different buyer). The relevant competitive set is cohort-format peers at similar price points, where the asymmetry is **mild to severe (roughly 2:1 to 6:1)**. This means a hybrid Tier 1–2 organic stack is the primary vehicle, with selective Tier 3 amplification of proven organic winners — but you are not in a spending race with Udemy, and treating Udemy's budget as the reference point would route you to the wrong game entirely. You compete on trust and specificity, not reach.

---

## 3a. Message-Market Fit Gate

**Question 1 — Revenue/commitment signal:** Have ≥10 people in the target audience paid, signed up, or directly asked for this without your prompting?
**Answer from brief: Yes.** 15 students completed the pilot cohort. 9 of 15 came via referral, indicating authentic demand pull. **Pass.**

**Question 2 — Language signal:** Can you name a specific pain the audience articulates in their own words before you pitch them?
**Answer from brief: Yes (inferred).** The brief states the audience is "feeling pressure to adopt AI tooling but wary of hype." This is a precise tension — not abstract interest in AI, but a specific combination of felt urgency and distrust of the available options. If the founder has heard this exact phrase or variants of it in DMs or conversations (likely given 4k newsletter subscribers and 14k LinkedIn followers), the language signal is present. **Pass — with a note: the founder should capture 5–10 verbatim quotes from pilot alumni or newsletter replies describing their pre-course state, and use this language in all campaign copy. Do not write the pain; transcribe it.**

**Question 3 — Close signal:** If you asked 5 people in the target audience to commit today (at €1,200), would at least 3 say yes?
**Answer from brief: Yes (strong signal).** 9/15 pilot referrals converted — a 60% warm-audience conversion rate. **Pass.**

**MMF verdict: 3/3 — confirmed.** This is a distribution problem. The product works, demand exists, the language of pain is known. Proceed to Stage 4.

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function: Trust compounding** — the bottleneck is "engineers find her eventually, but the audience she needs to reach doesn't follow her yet and needs a trust signal before they consider a €1,200 investment."

**Sector rider applied: Rider 1 (Cohort-Based Education)** — the room is the product; referral flywheels compound through the attendee network; community nodes are the single highest-leverage channel in this sector. Market the room, not the curriculum.

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Function | Weekly effort | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Founder LinkedIn (practitioner proof posts, documented workflow, "week in AI" series) | Tier 1 | Trust compounding | 2h | Founder |
| 2 | Newsletter (4k subs, 38% open) — weekly practitioner issues, cohort opens, alumni spotlights | Tier 1 | Trust compounding + conversion | 2h | Founder |
| 3 | Alumni referral program — structured outreach to 15 pilot alumni, recurring referral asks at cohort transitions | Tier 1 | Word of mouth | 1h | Ops |
| 4 | GitHub / OSS community — course-adjacent resources, annotated examples in the existing repo or a new public one | Tier 1 | Trust compounding + earned media | 1h | Founder |
| 5 | Earned media — podcast guesting and newsletter cross-posts targeting European engineer audiences | Tier 2 | Borrowed-audience trust + reach | 2h (ops research) + 1h founder prep | Ops + Founder |
| 6 | Long-tail SEO / content — practitioner-voice articles targeting intent queries ("AI for senior engineers," "practical LLM tooling for engineers") | Tier 2 | Demand capture | 2h | Ops |
| 7 | Boosting top-performing LinkedIn posts (24–48h organic traction gate required) | Tier 3 | Paid amplification of proven winners only | Capped at €500/month; not a default action | Ops |
| 8 | LinkedIn retargeting to newsletter subscribers and website visitors | Tier 3 | Warm retargeting | Capped at €300/month; only if organic produces a waiting audience | Ops |

**Channels deliberately excluded:**
- Broad cold Meta / LinkedIn ads: wrong function for this bottleneck; would burn budget on engineers who have no trust basis for a €1,200 commitment from an ad.
- TikTok: audience concentration mismatch for senior European engineers in a work context (unless the founder's voice is naturally TikTok-compatible; if so, revisit).
- Udemy / Coursera promotional listings: these attract price-sensitive buyers; the course competes on trust and peer quality, not discovery search volume.

---

## 5. 70/30 Allocation

**Organic 75% / Paid 25%** — leaning toward the organic end of mild-asymmetry allocation.

Rationale: the bottleneck is trust, not reach. The existing newsletter (38% open rate) and LinkedIn (14k followers) are already outperforming any cold paid channel on a cost-per-trust basis. The €2,500/month budget is most efficiently deployed as:

- **~€1,900/month → organic (founder time + ops time):** newsletter production, LinkedIn content, earned media outreach, alumni referral management, GitHub content.
- **~€600/month → paid:** no broad cold paid. All paid spend reserved for Tier 3 — boosting proven organic winners and warm retargeting of engaged audiences. If organic produces no traction-gated winners in a given month, the paid allocation stays unspent.

Monthly paid budget envelope: €600, with a hard rule that no single post gets boosted unless it has cleared the 24–48h organic traction gate (saves, shares, thread-depth comments — not raw likes).

**Total budget across 8 months: €4,800 paid (out of €20,000 total budget).** The remaining €15,200 is reserved for operations: landing page / application tooling, analytics, email platform, and one paid earned-media research sprint (see Section 8). If cohort 1 produces surplus revenue, a limited geo-targeted retargeting campaign for cohort 2 applications can be considered — subject to a lift test.

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

**Competitor category 1: Large course platforms (Udemy/Coursera and equivalents)**

(a) What they saturate: Paid search and affiliate channels with high-volume, low-price content. Constant retargeting ads promoting "AI for everyone" courses with stock-photography creative, celebrity-instructor branding, and completion certificates. Message: AI is for everyone, €20–50 will get you there, enroll now. Platform-reported engagement metrics drive their optimization; the content optimizes for enrollment volume, not outcome quality.

(b) The absence that becomes your signal: Their saturation of the "affordable, for everyone, learn fast" message creates an unclaimed space — the premium, small-cohort, practitioner-grade product for engineers who already know enough to know what they don't know. Your refusal to compete on price, on volume, or on "learn AI in 30 days" promises is the signal. A senior engineer scrolling past the twentieth "Complete AI Bootcamp" ad and then encountering a post that reads "Week 4 of using Copilot for real production work: here is where it failed and what I did instead" experiences something completely different.

(c) Positioning line: *"We sell the room — 40 practitioners who already know how to code, learning together what AI actually changes about their craft."*

---

**Competitor category 2: Cohort courses from other individual instructors (US-based, $2k+, paid LinkedIn/ad funnels)**

(a) What they saturate: Paid LinkedIn funnels, affiliate partnerships with developer newsletters, and polished launch-week content with social proof screenshots and waiting-list FOMO mechanics. Email sequences built on scarcity language. Testimonials curated for maximum credential signal ("Senior Staff Engineer at [FAANG]"). The message: trust me because others with impressive titles trust me, and spots are limited.

(b) The absence that becomes your signal: Their saturation of credential-based social proof and scarcity-launch mechanics creates an opening for process-based trust — the founder showing her actual work, including failures, rather than curated success signals. A senior engineer is more persuaded by seeing someone get something wrong and recover correctly than by a testimonial from someone whose job title is impressive. The absence of affiliate links and launch-week countdown timers is itself positioning: "we do not need the pressure mechanic because the people who completed the pilot cohort are asking when the next one opens."

(c) Positioning line: *"We show our work. The curriculum is public, the alumni are reachable, and the founder's code is on GitHub."*

---

**Unified campaign positioning line (holds across all content):**
*"Practical AI-assisted engineering, taught by someone who does it — not just teaches it."*

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

### Shape 1 — Community-First

**Build a free practitioner community before advertising a paid product.**

Launch a Slack or Discord for European senior engineers navigating AI adoption. Founder posts daily for 30 days. The community is the proof that she attracts peers, not just students. Cohort applications come from within the community and from community members' referrals outward.

**Self / Us / Now flagship content:**
- *Self:* "I've been a senior engineer for 12 years. When GitHub Copilot shipped, I spent three months convincing myself it was a toy. Then I shipped something in half the time I expected and didn't know what to do with that feeling."
- *Us:* "Most of us have now touched the tooling. A few of us have changed how we work. Almost none of us have talked to a peer who is honest about both what's better and what's worse."
- *Now:* "I'm opening a free community for engineers who want to have that conversation — no hype, no 'AI will replace you,' no oversell. Join this week before the first live session on [date]."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pro: Creates an owned, compounding audience before cohort 1 opens. Every community member is a warm prospect and a potential referrer. Credibility is observable, not asserted.
- Con: Requires daily founder presence for 30+ days — the single biggest time commitment of any shape. If the community does not reach escape velocity (20+ active weekly posters) within 6 weeks, it becomes a distraction rather than an asset. Risk: founder is also full-time teaching during cohorts, which compresses community time.
- Best fit: Choose this shape if the founder can commit 1–1.5h/day for the first 30 days and has bandwidth to hand off community moderation to 2–3 engaged early members before cohort 1 starts.

---

### Shape 2 — Earned-Media First

**One newsworthy practitioner artifact drives press + organic amplification before cohort 1 opens.**

The artifact: a rigorous, publicly published analysis of AI pair-programming tooling for senior engineers — not a list, not a guide, but an actual comparative evaluation the founder ran on her own production code. Something engineers share in Slack channels because it is useful, not because it is promotional. This anchors the founder's authority in a single piece that can be referenced for years.

**Self / Us / Now flagship content** (for a LinkedIn thread version of the same artifact):
- *Self:* "I ran four AI coding tools on the same set of real tasks over 30 days. I logged every session. Here are the results — including the one that made me worse at my job for two weeks before it made me better."
- *Us:* "If you've tried any of these tools and wondered whether you were using them wrong or they were just not ready yet — you're asking the right question. Most benchmarks don't test what we actually care about."
- *Now:* "I'm sharing the full evaluation doc and the task log. Link in the first comment. And if you want to run this kind of experiment with 39 other senior engineers: that's what the next cohort is."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pro: The artifact does marketing indefinitely — it gets bookmarked, referenced, and shared by engineers who never see an ad. Zero paid spend required. The natural distribution channel (engineer Slack communities, Hacker News, LinkedIn threads) is exactly the founder's target audience.
- Con: Requires significant upfront time investment to produce something genuinely rigorous (not just useful-looking). A mediocre artifact backfires by signaling lower standards than the course implies. Earned media outreach requires research and warm relationships; cold pitches without a hook fail.
- Best fit: Choose this shape if the founder has a specific recent experience — a real project where AI tooling changed the outcome — that she has not published yet. The specificity is the authenticity; a manufactured comparison is not this shape.

---

### Shape 3 — Search-Capture First

**Dominate the long-tail intent queries senior engineers use when evaluating AI upskilling investment.**

Produce 4–6 long-form practitioner articles over the 8-month window, each targeting a specific high-intent, low-competition query the cohort's target audience types into Google or into an LLM. Examples (use these as structural templates, not verbatim keywords — research actual search volume):
- "How AI tooling actually changes a senior engineer's workflow"
- "Practical AI pair programming: what works after the demo ends"
- "Is AI coding assistance worth €1,200 of training investment"

Each article links to the newsletter signup and the cohort application. Newsletter subscribers become the warm audience for cohort opens. The search function runs continuously between cohort launches, compounding over the 8-month window.

**Self / Us / Now flagship content** (newsletter version of the top article):
- *Self:* "Two years ago I would have said 'AI coding tools are for people who don't know the fundamentals.' Here's the moment I realized I was wrong, and what I did next."
- *Us:* "Most senior engineers I know have now tried at least one of these tools. The ones who've actually changed how they work are a small subset. The difference isn't the tool — it's having enough context to integrate it without losing what already works."
- *Now:* "If you want the longer version of that integration process — with the mistakes — it's the core of what we build together in the next cohort. Applications open [date]."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Pro: Compounds over time. Articles written in month 1 bring in cohort 3 applications. Ops-executable with founder review; lower founder time commitment than community-first. Best fit for the 8h/week founder capacity constraint.
- Con: Slowest shape to produce initial applications. SEO takes 6–12 weeks to produce measurable organic traffic; the first cohort open may not benefit much from articles published at campaign start. Requires keyword research investment in week 1.
- Best fit: Choose this shape as the **primary organic infrastructure** running in parallel with either Shape 1 or Shape 2 — not as a standalone. The articles anchor search and LLM-cited authority; the community or earned-media shape generates the near-term cohort 1 applications.

---

**Recommended combination:** Shape 2 (earned-media first, one flagship artifact) as the cohort 1 launch vehicle, combined with Shape 3 (search-capture articles built out by ops over months 2–8) as the compounding infrastructure, and Shape 1 (community) held as a month 3–4 investment after cohort 1 produces 40 alumni with demonstrated outcomes.

---

## 8. First-30-Days Action List

**Capacity envelope:**
- Founder: 8h/week marketing. During cohorts, this is non-negotiable; do not exceed.
- Ops: 10h/week.
- Total per week: 18h.

**Weeks 1–4 action plan (pre-cohort 1 launch):**

### Week 1 — Research and foundation

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Earned media target research:** Produce a ranked list of 15 podcasts, newsletters, and communities where European senior engineers congregate. Criteria: host/editor is in the target audience, has published on AI tooling in the past 6 months, takes guest contributors. Deliverable: ranked list with RSS feed, contact channel, and recent episode/issue most relevant to a pitch hook. | Ops | 4h | Ranked list of 15 targets delivered by Friday |
| **Alumni activation:** Email all 15 pilot alumni with a specific ask — share one LinkedIn post this week describing what they built during the pilot cohort and tag the founder. Provide a draft post they can edit (not a template that reads like a template — a draft in their voice based on their cohort feedback). | Ops | 2h | At least 5 alumni post within 7 days |
| **Cohort 1 application page live:** Landing page with: the one-sentence positioning line, 3 alumni testimonials using verbatim language from their feedback, curriculum outline (can be sparse — what the room delivers, not just topics), and application form. No countdown timer. No "only 3 spots left." | Ops | 3h | Page live by Wednesday; founder approves copy |
| **Founder practitioner post #1 — LinkedIn:** The "I was wrong" story (see Shape 2 Self/Us/Now above). Real project, real moment, real diffs. First-person. No brand mentions. CTA: link to newsletter signup in first comment. | Founder | 1h | Post live by Thursday; measure saves + shares + thread comments at 48h |

Week 1 total: Founder 1h, Ops 9h. Within envelope.

---

### Week 2 — First earned-media push

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Earned media outreach — batch 1 (5 targets):** Using the week-1 ranked list, pitch the top 5 targets. Each pitch must include: (a) one sentence on why this specific host/editor would find this useful for their audience, referencing their recent episode/issue by name; (b) a specific proposed topic ("30-minute conversation on what happens to a senior engineer's workflow 90 days after adopting AI tooling — I tracked mine"); (c) a link to the practitioner post from week 1 as a sample of voice. Send Tuesday morning via email. | Ops | 3h | At least 2 replies within 7 days (booking or "not now but here's why") |
| **Newsletter issue — practitioner proof:** The full version of the week-1 LinkedIn post, expanded. Include: the specific task, the tool failure, what you did instead, and the one lesson that would have saved you two weeks. CTA: cohort 1 application link. | Founder | 2h | Issue sent by Wednesday; measure open rate and click-through to application page (UTM-tagged) |
| **UTM tracking live across all channels:** Every link in the newsletter, every LinkedIn post CTA, every alumni referral post — tagged. utm_source=newsletter / linkedin / alumni-referral, utm_medium=organic, utm_campaign=cohort-1. | Ops | 1h | All links tagged before week 2 newsletter goes out |
| **GitHub: publish practitioner artifact draft:** The comparative evaluation artifact (Shape 2) — even a partial version — posted as a public repo or Gist. One LinkedIn post pointing to it ("I've been running this evaluation for 30 days. Here's what I have so far. Full results in 2 weeks."). | Founder | 2h | Repo live; post published; measure GitHub stars and LinkedIn thread depth |
| **Cohort 1 applications: review and reply personally to all applicants within 48h** | Founder | 1h/week ongoing | Applicants receive a personal reply, not an autoresponder |

Week 2 total: Founder 5h, Ops 4h. Within envelope.

---

### Week 3 — Amplification and alumni follow-up

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Organic traction gate check:** Review week-1 LinkedIn post and week-2 GitHub post. If either has generated genuine organic signal (saves, shares, thread-depth comments from non-followers), it is eligible for a boost. Maximum €200 boost, targeted to: 1st and 2nd degree LinkedIn connections of the founder + website custom audience (if pixel is installed). Do not boost if the organic signal is weak. | Ops | 1h | Decision made with evidence, not default |
| **Earned media outreach — batch 2 (5 more targets):** Second group from the ranked list. Same pitch format. | Ops | 2h | Track reply rate vs. batch 1 |
| **LinkedIn post #2 — the room pitch:** "What a cohort of 40 senior engineers actually does together, week by week. Not a course. Not a lecture series. Here's what the pilot cohort built." Include one alumni quote (with name and company, with permission). Link: newsletter signup. | Founder | 1h | Measure shares and inbound DMs from non-followers |
| **Alumni referral — second touch:** DM to any pilot alumni who did not post in week 1. Single message, no guilt, specific alternative ask: "Even a reply to this message with one sentence about what you built would help — I'll share it as a newsletter section with your name." | Ops | 1h | 3+ additional alumni engaged |
| **Publish full practitioner evaluation artifact:** The complete comparative evaluation published on GitHub + newsletter issue featuring it. LinkedIn thread: 5 key findings with full doc linked. | Founder | 2h | Track LinkedIn saves, shares, thread depth, newsletter signups from artifact (UTM) |

Week 3 total: Founder 3h, Ops 4h. Within envelope.

---

### Week 4 — Cohort 1 open + conversion focus

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cohort 1 formally open — announcement newsletter issue:** Personal, first-person. "Here's what I learned from the pilot. Here's what's different about cohort 1. Here's who this is for and who it is not for. Applications close [date]." Include: 2 alumni testimonials (verbatim), the curriculum outline link, and the application form link. | Founder | 2h | Open rate ≥38% (baseline); application click-through rate via UTM |
| **LinkedIn announcement post:** Same message, conversational format. Avoid the word "excited." Real language. Real specifics. | Founder | 1h | Measure saves, shares, application inquiries from LinkedIn |
| **Follow up with earned media — any warm replies from weeks 2–3:** If any podcast/newsletter has replied, move to scheduling. If not, one follow-up to unreplied pitches (single email, one sentence: "Happy to do a shorter format or swap content if that fits better"). | Ops | 1h | At least 1 booking confirmed |
| **Application review and personal response:** Every applicant gets a personal reply within 24h. Rejection (if any) is specific: "This cohort is for X; you're describing Y — here's why I think a different path might serve you better." | Founder | 2h | 100% response rate within 24h |
| **Produce 30-day measurement report:** Per-channel conversion rate (UTM data): newsletter, LinkedIn, alumni referral, GitHub, earned media. Rank channels by conversion rate per unique visitor. Identify top channel and weakest channel. Decision: double effort on top, drop or reframe weakest. | Ops | 2h | Report delivered to founder; next 30 days adjusted |

Week 4 total: Founder 5h, Ops 3h. Within envelope.

**Capacity check:** Peak week (week 4) is Founder 5h, Ops 3h. Well within 8h and 10h envelopes. The week-2 newsletter + GitHub artifact week is the heaviest founder week at 5h — acceptable. No cuts required. If cohort teaching overlaps with any of these weeks, ops absorbs the research and outreach tasks; founder delivers only the content that requires her voice (posts, newsletter, application replies).

**Community-build timing note:** The Slack/Discord community build (Shape 1) is deliberately deferred to month 3 or after cohort 1. Reason: seeding a community while the founder is full-time preparing and delivering cohort 1 will produce a dead room. Launch the community after cohort 1 alumni can seed it with real outcomes. The community becomes the cohort 2 and 3 marketing engine.

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

**Paid applies in a limited capacity (Tier 3 only, €600/month envelope).**

**Creative direction:** All paid-amplified content must originate from organic posts that have cleared the 24–48h traction gate. No boosting purpose-built ad creative. The boost extends the life of content that already earned organic trust; it does not manufacture trust for content that didn't.

**Voice rules for organic content (which becomes the ad):**
- First person, founder's voice only — not "we," not "the course," not "the team."
- Specific details: actual numbers, actual task descriptions, actual mistakes. Vague language is the death of conversion for this audience.
- No scarcity pressure. Senior engineers with €60–100k salaries who are evaluating a €1,200 professional investment are repelled by countdown timers and "only 3 spots left" copy. Respect their intelligence.
- No certification language unless the founder is genuinely offering one with external recognition. "Certificate of completion" from a course most people haven't heard of is noise.

**24–48h organic traction gate — hard rule:**
Do not boost any post until it has demonstrated, in the first 24–48 hours, at least two of: (a) saves that exceed 1% of reach, (b) shares from people outside the founder's 1st-degree network, (c) comments that advance the discussion rather than just affirming it, (d) inbound DMs from the post's CTA. Likes are not a gate signal.

**Audience targeting for any boosted post:**
1. LinkedIn: custom audience of newsletter subscribers (upload email list) + 1st/2nd degree connections of the founder. No cold interest-based targeting.
2. Website retargeting: anyone who visited the application page and did not submit. Budget: €100–150/month. Frequency cap: 3 impressions in 7 days.
3. Lookalike audiences: only after cohort 1 produces 40 confirmed applicants (enough to build a meaningful lookalike). Not before.

**Do not bid on the founder's own name as a brand keyword** without running Template 4 (brand-keyword holdout) first. The organic result for the founder's name likely outranks any paid result given her existing LinkedIn and GitHub presence; paying for those clicks is cannibalism.

**Frequency cap:** 5 impressions per person per creative before refreshing. Creative rotation cycle: 2 weeks. If no new organic winner has cleared the traction gate in a given 2-week window, do not refresh with manufactured content — skip the paid cycle for that window.

**If a cohort fails to fill:** Do not respond by increasing paid spend. Run the MMF gate check again — is the curriculum, price, or timing wrong? Does the application page copy accurately describe who the cohort is for? The failure is almost certainly upstream of distribution.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template 5 — Organic-source attribution (primary, zero-budget version)**
**Template 4 (micro-lift, supplementary) — for any paid amplification spend**

### Template 5: Organic-source attribution

**Hypothesis:** Among the organic channels in the stack (newsletter, LinkedIn founder posts, alumni referrals, GitHub artifact, earned media), conversion rate per unique visitor differs materially by source. Identifying and doubling down on the top-converting channel will produce more applications per hour of founder effort than equal effort across all channels.

**Design:**
- UTM-tag every link from day 1 (utm_source, utm_medium=organic, utm_campaign=cohort-1 through cohort-3).
- Conversion event: application form submitted (not started — submitted).
- Analytics: Plausible or PostHog with UTM source tracking. Do not use GA4 unless already in place; both Plausible and PostHog provide UTM breakdowns without sampling.
- Window: 30 days minimum per cohort cycle (8-month campaign spans 3 measurement cycles).
- Rank channels by: applications per unique visitor per source.

**Decision rule:**
- Top channel: double weekly effort in next 30-day cycle.
- Any channel below 20% of top channel's conversion rate: drop unless it serves a secondary function (SEO backlink, credibility, borrowed-audience trust) the conversion metric cannot see.
- If overall conversion rate across all channels falls below 0.5%: re-run MMF gate. The bottleneck has shifted upstream.

**Caveat (say this out loud):** This is directional, not causal. Multi-touch attribution is underspecified — an engineer who applies via the newsletter link may have first heard about the course from an alumni referral. Treat the rankings as effort-allocation guidance, not proof of lift. If paid spend increases in cohort 2–3, graduate to Template 1 (geo-holdout) for real lift measurement.

### Micro-lift supplement (for any paid boost spend)

**Hypothesis:** Boosting a specific LinkedIn post (post X, cleared the traction gate) produces incremental application submissions above the organic baseline from that post.

**Design:**
- Ask in the application form: "How did you first hear about this course?" with options including "saw a post on LinkedIn," "newsletter," "referred by a colleague," and "other."
- During a boost period: compare self-reported LinkedIn attribution rate vs. the organic-only baseline period (week before boost).
- If <5% of applicants credit a LinkedIn ad/post during boost period: the boost is not producing incremental applications. Stop boosting that creative.

**Decision threshold:**
- Incremental applications credited to the boost > €50/application (i.e., a €200 boost should produce at least 4 additional applications that would not have come otherwise). If not: stop and reallocate to organic.

**What to track across the 8-month campaign:**

| Metric | Measurement cadence | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Applications submitted per cohort, by UTM source | Per application (real-time) | Application form + Plausible/PostHog |
| Newsletter open rate and click-through to application page | Per issue | Email platform (UTM-tagged links) |
| LinkedIn post saves and shares (organic traction gate) | 48h after each post | LinkedIn native analytics |
| Alumni referral conversion: alumni posts → inbound applications | Weekly | CRM tag or manual tracking |
| Earned media: podcast/newsletter appearances → newsletter signups and applications | Per appearance (UTM) | Plausible/PostHog |
| Cohort fill rate per cohort (seats filled / seats available) | Per cohort cycle | Application tracker |
| Referral % of new applicants (replicating pilot's 60% referral rate) | Per cohort | Application form question |

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

### Track these

| Metric | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Applications submitted (by UTM source) | The only leading indicator that directly predicts cohort fill |
| Cohort fill rate (seats filled / 40) | The campaign's actual goal |
| Newsletter click-through rate to application page | Measures whether newsletter content is converting intent, not just being read |
| LinkedIn post saves + shares from non-followers | Distinguishes organic trust reach from echo-chamber engagement |
| Alumni referral rate (% of applicants who name a prior student) | The highest-trust, lowest-cost channel; tracking it tells you whether the room is delivering on its promise |
| Earned media conversions (UTM-tracked newsletter signups + applications from podcast/newsletter appearances) | Whether earned-media effort is producing distribution |
| Application-to-enrollment conversion rate | Are accepted applicants completing payment? If this drops, the problem is post-acceptance, not top-of-funnel |
| Referral % at cohort 2 and 3 (target: maintain the pilot's 60%) | The alumni network is the long-term engine; watch for decay |

### Ignore these

| Metric | Why it lies |
|---|---|
| LinkedIn follower count | A follower who never reads a post is worth less than a newsletter subscriber who forwards every issue. Follower count says nothing about trust or conversion. |
| Impressions and CPM | Reach above the first 5–6 impressions does not persuade — it annoys. Impression volume is not correlated with application volume in a high-consideration purchase. |
| Post likes | Likes are the cheapest engagement signal and the most easily gamed. They are not a traction gate signal. They are not a proxy for trust. Do not make decisions based on them. |
| Platform-reported ROAS on any boosted post | Attribution is not causality. The platform fires on every conversion from someone who saw an ad in the last N days. Incremental lift requires a holdout. The micro-lift supplement in Section 10 is the right measurement tool. |
| Website session count without conversion segmentation | Sessions without UTM source breakdown are noise. 10,000 sessions from a viral post that produces 0 applications is worse than 200 sessions from the newsletter that produces 8. |
| Social media engagement rate as a vanity number | An overall engagement rate without segmentation by content type, audience, and conversion outcome tells you nothing actionable. |
| Waitlist size without a payment signal | A waitlist with no payment or deposit commitment is a list of people who thought "maybe." It is not demand. Do not count it as cohort fill until money moves. |

**Standing warning from Rider 1 (Cohort-Based Education):** The most common failure mode in this sector is selling information in a world where information is free. Every vanity metric in the "ignore" list is a proxy for information-product metrics (impressions, reach, views). The actual product is the room — the peers, the accountability, the direct feedback. Track the metrics that reflect room quality: referral rate, alumni NPS that translates to word-of-mouth introductions, cohort-to-alumni network growth. If the room is delivering, the campaign takes care of itself. If the room is not delivering, no campaign fixes it.
